title: normalizeEncoderOptions spreads the full options object and rebuilds two arrays on every encode call
description: Every call to createEncoder (or adaptBindingCreators.createEncoder) calls normalizeEncoderOptions, which does `[...(opts.advancedFrameSettings ?? [])]` to copy the entire advancedFrameSettings array, then `extraChannels.map(...)` to copy the extraChannels array, then `{ ...opts }` to shallow-clone the entire EncoderOptions object. For single-shot toy images this is trivial, but for batch/animation pipelines that call createEncoder in a loop (e.g., pyramid-ingest) these three allocations happen every invocation. The extraChannels map also uses a non-null-asserted `opts.extraChannelPlanes![i]` inside a loop.

title: Linear scan over advancedFrameSettings with Array.some() on every convenience-field lowering
description: normalizeEncoderOptions iterates over up to 6 convenience fields (progressiveDc, groupOrder, modular, modularOptions.*,  brotliEffort). For each one that is set it calls `adv.some(x => x.id === id)` which is a full linear scan over the existing advancedFrameSettings array. With N convenience fields and M existing settings this is O(N*M) per encode call. While N and M are both small and bounded, it fires on every single encode invocation.

title: loadNativeBinding called redundantly on every createDecoder / createEncoder invocation but only cached for the non-custom case
description: The convenience functions createDecoder and createEncoder (lines 355-361) each call createNativeCodecFacade(loadNativeBinding()) on every call. loadNativeBinding is cached (cachedBinding), so the cost is a null check plus one function call per encode/decode operation — cheap but not free. The larger cost is that createNativeCodecFacade itself always constructs a fresh closure object `{ createDecoder(...) {...}, createEncoder(...) {...} }` each time. Callers that invoke createDecoder in a tight loop would pay this allocation every call.

title: wrapDecoder allocates a new Promise and three async closures on every decoder construction
description: wrapDecoder creates a fresh `new Promise<void>` plus the `release` closure plus at least three async wrapper functions (close, cancel, dispose) and two async generator functions (events, seekToFrame shim, seekToTime shim) for every NativeDecoder instance. For code paths that create many short-lived decoders (e.g. batch thumbnail generation, pyramid-ingest), this is six–eight allocation sites per decode. The inputDone Promise in particular cannot be reused because it is per-decoder state.

title: Multiple test cases each call readFileSync on index.ts to check for string presence — repeated synchronous disk reads
description: codec.test.ts has at least 7 test cases in three describe blocks that each call `readFileSync(new URL('../src/index.ts', import.meta.url), 'utf8')` independently. Each call does a full synchronous filesystem read of the entire index.ts source file for a simple string-contains check. These reads are redundant — the file content is identical across all calls. A module-level shared constant would reduce 7 disk reads to 1.

title: facade.test.ts reads native.cc source synchronously inside each test instead of at module load
description: Two test cases (lines 108-113 and 115-120) in facade.test.ts each call `readFileSync(new URL('../src/native.cc', import.meta.url), 'utf8')` independently. native.cc is a large C++ file; reading it twice per test suite run is wasteful. The content should be read once at module scope and reused.

title: fileExists uses synchronous accessSync in the module's binary resolution path
description: resolveSourceBinary calls fileExists twice (for Release and Debug paths), each using accessSync which is synchronous filesystem I/O. This runs on module load inside loadNativeBinding (which is called synchronously from createDecoder/createEncoder). While this is a one-time cost due to cachedBinding, the synchronous I/O blocks the event loop during the first call. An async version with fs.access would be non-blocking, though refactoring loadNativeBinding to be async is a larger change.

title: No encode/decode throughput benchmarks exist for the native codec path
description: All tests in codec.test.ts exercise correctness only (round-trip, extra channels, animation). There is no timing measurement, no throughput baseline, and no regression gate for the native libjxl encode/decode performance. Given that the native path is the high-performance alternative to WASM, a missing benchmark means encode/decode regressions (e.g. from build flag changes like the documented /O2 omission) will not be caught until a human notices slow wall-clock behavior.
